cards.front: What two things do natural hazards pose major risks to?
cards.explanation: The specification states that natural hazards pose major risks to both people and property.

choices.text: People and property.
choices.feedback: Correct: both are named as facing major risks.

choices.text: People only.
choices.feedback: Property is also named, so this is incomplete.

choices.text: Property only.
choices.feedback: People are also named, so this is incomplete.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Natural hazards pose major risks to people and
property.

cards.front: Which six causes of desertification on the fringes of hot deserts does AQA name?
cards.back: Climate change, population growth, removal of fuel wood, overgrazing, over-cultivation and soil erosion.
cards.explanation: The specification lists these six causes after stating that areas on hot-desert fringes are at risk. Water and soil management, tree planting and appropriate technology are listed separately as risk-reduction strategies.

choices.text: Climate change, population growth, removal of fuel wood, overgrazing, over-cultivation and soil erosion.
choices.feedback: Correct: all six listed causes are included.

choices.text: Water and soil management, tree planting and use of appropriate technology.
choices.feedback: These are strategies used to reduce the risk, not the listed causes.

choices.text: Climate change, population growth, water and soil management, tree planting and appropriate technology.
choices.feedback: This mixes two listed causes with risk-reduction strategies and omits four causes.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Areas on the fringe of hot deserts are at risk of
desertification.
Causes of desertification – climate change,
population growth, removal of fuel wood,
overgrazing, over-cultivation and soil erosion.
Strategies used to reduce the risk of
desertification – water and soil management,
tree planting and use of appropriate technology.

cards.front: Which two needs are strategies in cold environments used to balance?
cards.explanation: AQA frames the task as balancing economic development with conservation in cold environments and names technology, governments, international agreements and conservation groups as approaches.

choices.text: Economic development and conservation.
choices.feedback: Correct: both needs must be balanced.

choices.text: Economic development only.
choices.feedback: This drops conservation, one of the two needs to be balanced.

choices.text: Conservation only.
choices.feedback: This drops economic development, one of the two needs to be balanced.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Strategies used to balance the needs of
economic development and conservation in
cold environments – use of technology, role of
governments, international agreements and
conservation groups.

cards.front: What must an overview of UK physical landscapes locate?
cards.back: Major upland and lowland areas, and river systems.
cards.explanation: The required overview concerns the locations of major upland and lowland areas and river systems.

choices.text: Major upland and lowland areas, and river systems.
choices.feedback: Correct: the overview includes all three.

choices.text: Major upland and lowland areas only.
choices.feedback: This omits river systems from the required overview.

choices.text: River systems only.
choices.feedback: This omits the major upland and lowland areas.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
The UK has a range of diverse landscapes. An overview of the location of major upland/
lowland areas and river systems.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 14-14; 3.1.3.1 UK physical landscapes

cards.front: When designing fieldwork data collection sheets and collecting data, which considerations does AQA require students to understand?
cards.back: Accuracy, sample size and procedures, control groups and reliability.
cards.explanation: The design-and-collection requirement explicitly links fieldwork to understanding accuracy, sample size and procedures, control groups and reliability.

choices.text: Accuracy, sample size and procedures, control groups and reliability.
choices.feedback: Correct: these are the considerations attached to fieldwork design and data collection.

choices.text: Proportion and ratio, magnitude and frequency.
choices.feedback: These are separate numerical relationships to use, not the considerations tied to fieldwork design and collection.

choices.text: Drawing informed conclusions from numerical data.
choices.feedback: Drawing conclusions is a separate numerical skill; it does not replace the listed design-and-collection considerations.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
• design fieldwork data collection sheets and collect data with an understanding of accuracy,
sample size and procedures, control groups and reliability
• understand and correctly use proportion and ratio, magnitude and frequency
• draw informed conclusions from numerical data.

cards.front: According to AQA, qualitative and quantitative geographical data should come from which source categories?
cards.explanation: The specification pairs qualitative and quantitative data with both primary and secondary sources.

choices.text: Both primary and secondary sources.
choices.feedback: Correct: the requirement includes both source categories.

choices.text: Primary sources only.
choices.feedback: Secondary sources are also included in the requirement.

choices.text: Secondary sources only.
choices.feedback: Primary sources are also included in the requirement.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Use of qualitative and quantitative data from both primary and secondary sources to obtain,
illustrate, communicate, interpret, analyse and evaluate geographical information.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 30-32; 3.4.5 Use of qualitative and quantitative data

cards.front: In AQA Geography, what two qualities should conclusions about geographical questions and issues have?
cards.back: They should be well-evidenced and informed.
cards.explanation: The specification explicitly requires well-evidenced and informed conclusions about geographical questions and issues.

choices.text: They should be well-evidenced and informed.
choices.feedback: Correct: these are the two stated qualities of conclusions.

choices.text: They should be descriptive and analytical.
choices.feedback: These are writing modes from another requirement, not the two qualities named for conclusions.

choices.text: They should be extended and effectively communicated.
choices.feedback: This combines requirements for arguments and communicating ideas, not the qualities specified for conclusions.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Students should demonstrate the ability to:
• identify questions and sequences of enquiry
• write descriptively, analytically and critically
• communicate their ideas effectively
• develop an extended written argument
• draw well-evidenced and informed conclusions about geographical questions and issues.
cards.sourceLocator: Official PDF pages 30-32; 3.4.6 Formulate enquiry and argument

cards.front: According to AQA's Geography literacy requirement, how should students communicate information?
cards.back: In ways suitable for a range of target audiences.
cards.explanation: Most communication is through the written word, but the requirement is broader: information should be communicated in ways suitable for a range of target audiences.

choices.text: In ways suitable for a range of target audiences.
choices.feedback: Correct: communication must be suitable for a range of target audiences.

choices.text: Only through the written word.
choices.feedback: The source says most communication is written, not that all communication must be.

choices.text: In ways suitable for one fixed target audience.
choices.feedback: The requirement specifies a range of target audiences, not just one.

cards.sourceExcerpt:
Most communication is through the written word, raising the importance of good literacy skills.
Students should be able to communicate information in ways suitable for a range of target
audiences.
